All right, you're back to script building in the next lesson. Let's move on to the bare of a topic we have in hand, which is an overarching equation that sits atop the entire straight line system and governs whether or not you end up closing and it's called energy in benefits out. Let me explain what I mean. You go through a wholesale and you make this airtight logical case about why they should love your product, trust and connect with you, trust and connect with the company, right? Make an emotional case for the same thing, right? You go through a whole thing and then you get to the point where you're about to ask for the order and imagine you say this. Say John, let me tell you how you get started here. Okay, what I need you to do is fill out this form here. I want your name, then give me your birth date. I want your exact social security number. Then I want you to go down to the bank, make a copy of your license and then take that, put in a self-addressed stamped envelope, send it to me, then go to the bank, get it notarized by someone else there. Then when you're done with that, I want you to go back to your house, jump through two rings of fire, one hoop of fire, and then when you're done, then you get the product. Sound good? Now, I exaggerated a bit there, but not that much. See, this is where companies miss the boat so dramatically that it destroys their clothes. They do everything right. They make a great case. So let's say they usually don't do everything right, but let's say they do some things right. They're kind of in a decent position to close, but when they ask for the order, they make it incredibly complicated. The profits will expend a lot of energy to get the results. And what they do is they make it really difficult, lots of energy going out and they don't restate the benefits at that moment. So here's the equation. If in the moment when someone's making a decision, if the perceived benefits that someone's getting, the value of all the benefits, both now and in the long term, exceed the amount of energy they have to expend to get those benefits, they tend to buy. Conversely, if when they're about to say yes or no, they do that calculation, they say if the intended benefits, both short and long term, are less than the energy they have to expend, then they're going to say no. Makes sense? In other words, if the amount of benefits is less than the amount of energy, why would you do it? Now, just remember, this is an unconscious equation for the most part that doesn't rear its ugly head, so to speak, until that moment right after you ask for the order. And at that moment, right after you say sound good, in that moment, that's when the prospect unconsciously runs that whole equation to their mind and what it really sums up to at the end of the day is, is it worth it? Is it really worth it? Considering all this I've got to do to get these benefits, doesn't really make sense for me to buy. And if that answer is no, they won't buy. If the answer is yes, it doesn't mean that they will buy, but it opens up the possibility for them to buy. Does that make sense to you? And here's the deal, a company that doesn't spend it on anybody on the planet, Amazon.com, they have got this equation down to a science. What is it? One click buying. They make it so easy for you. You don't have to expend energy to put your hand in your pocket, take out your credit card. They have your information stored. You're a prime member. All you do is you go on, you see something you like, it shows you a picture, tells you what it does, you click, you own it, deliver it to your door. Massive benefits, easy to get, deliver it to you, you get the product, guaranteed, very little energy. Now, to that end though, what is the one energy that you are spending no matter what there? Besides that little click you make, there's one other thing. Tell you what it is. Money is actually energy. See, money itself is basically stored energy. You work really hard during the week, right? You get a paycheck, you get money for your hard work. Now we have our basic needs we have to fill. We have rent or a mortgage, we have our food bills, medical, our clothing, we have some entertainment as well, right? But at the end of the week, if you're doing well, you have some extra money left over, so you put that into the bank and that represents what? Stored energy. So now when you're about to buy something that you need, or you really want, so to speak, right, you're about to buy it, what do you say to them? Well, is it worth to expend that energy? So money is part of the equation. So after spend money, expend stored energy, and then what else? Do I have to go jump through those rings of fire in the hoop, so to speak, or is it very simple to get started? Now let's go back to that same scenario laid out. And if you go through the whole script, you do a great job, you build the airtight logical case, the airtight emotional case, but instead of doing that whole ring of fire stuff, you say, Jim, Jill, getting started is very simple. It's a question of some basic information. We do everything else for you, and you're going to get, remember, you get benefit A, benefit B, benefit C, and believe me, you'll be very glad you did this. Sound good? Well, if you do it like that, obviously, you have a much higher chance of closing someone. Because remember, you've stacked on those benefits at the very end and showed them how easy it is to get started. So low energy in, massive benefits out. And I guess another way to look at this is that when it comes to sort of the benefits that something gives you or any person you're selling to, we have very short memories for what those benefits will do for us. So you could have said all those benefits in the beginning, but by the time you ask for the order, the thought of expending energy must once again be rebalanced or exceeded by all those benefits. Not everyone listed out ad nauseam, but a summation, a quick summary, should show them that yes, overall is just definitely worth it. And that's your equation, energy in, benefits out. And by the way, I'll tell you a great story here. This one really applies to like a lot of service industries. I'll give you an example. I remember in the early modules, we spoke about this dentist, right? And had this cap and the whole nine yards, right? Well, here's the thing. When he was trying to get me to investigate my tooth, he goes, hey, can you tell me what kind of screw you have in your tooth? And I was like, screw you? Well, just imagine what he did. He was actually asking me to expend the energy, go out and go on the interlook up a call the doctor, find out what type of tooth I have, what screw I have in. I mean, hello, who's going to do that? The smart thing to do is a service provider say, listen, we do everything for you. We make it very easy. And what you're going to get is this benefit, that benefit, that benefit right now. Again, I don't want you to lie here. So in other words, there's going to be some work to do on the part of the prospect. Tell them what it is. Don't say, oh, it's so simple. They got to do eight things, right? Because like for instance, say in the banking industry, sometimes they have to go out and fill out an application. They got to, you know, get it notarized. There are some things in some cases that the prospect must do. Don't lie. My point is that you want to minimize them whenever possible and then balance that out by showing them all the amazing benefits they're going to get right before you ask for the order. Okay. Now again, this doesn't just apply when you ask for the order. It also applies when you are looping in the back half of the sale and you get these buy signals. Let's say someone says to you, say, okay, Jordan, how much does that cost? And I say, oh, it's after I've asked for the order now, I gave him the loop. What's that cost again? I say, oh, it's only $5,000. I just destroyed myself. Right? Think about that. I said, it's only $5,000. What's that? That's stored energy. What I should have said, oh, it's only $5,000. And again, what you're going to get is XYZ benefit, ABC benefit, DEF benefit, and believe me, the only problem you'll have is you didn't get started six months because you'd already be in a great situation. Does that sound fair enough? You always want to balance out your buy signals. You answer it by saying, yes, this is what it cost. And it's what you get. This benefit, that benefit, that benefit. I mean, you do this. Just like I promised you, this one thing is one of those good enough factors meeting that once you start doing this, your closing rate goes flying up straight away. And that, my friend, is the energy in benefits out equation. Now I've got a bunch of different questions for you in the quiz section. This is a really, really important one here. So take your time to go through this quiz. And when you're done, I'll see you on the other side.
